content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

January 27, 1992

Evet Abt, Chief
Deputy City Attorney
151 West Mission Street
San Jose, California 95110

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 91-541

Dear Ms. Abt:

You have requested advice regarding the disclosure and
disqualification obligations required by the Political Reform Act
(the "Act")1 for public officials who may receive parking permits
from the San Jose Airport. Since your advice seeks general
guidance specific to no particular individual, we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance.2

QUESTION

Are there reporting and disqualification obligations for
city, county, and Federal public officials who receive parking
permits for parking at the Airport while on official business?

CONCLUSION

Parking permits given to city officials for parking at the
airport while on official city business would not be considered
gifts and would be neither reportable nor relevant to conflict of
interest disqualification. Parking permits given to county or
state officials are reportable gifts to the recipients. Permits
given to Federal officials are not within the jurisdiction of the
Act.

__________________________

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 13000, et seq. All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs.
Section 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

FACTS

The Office of the City Attorney in the city of San Jose,
California ("City") is developing an ordinance regarding payment
of fees by persons parking at the Airport. The Airport is a City
department. The City is considering granting exemptions to
payment of parking fees to city, county, state, and federal
officials who park at the Airport while traveling on official
business. Your concern is whether receipt of such parking permits
would subject the recipient to disqualification and disqualification
provisions of the Act. At present, City officials who travel on
official business are reimbursed by the City for their parking
fees.

ANALYSIS

We have previously advised that parking passes given to
public officials are reportable gifts when they are given to the
official by a governmental entity other than the one that employs
the official. Bailey Advice Letter, I-91-424.

However, in the circumstances herein, the Airport is a City
department, and City officials who park at the Airport while
traveling on City business are reimbursed for any out-of-pocket
parking fees incurred. For City officials using the Airport
parking facilities while on official business, reimbursement for
parking is in the nature of repayment for a personal outlay of
funds to cover a cost that is related to the officials' duties.
Providing a permit for City officials to use while parking at the
Airport while on City business would serve the same purpose as the
current reimbursement procedure. The permit, therefore, would not
be a gift, and would be neither reportable nor relevant to
a conflict of interest analysis. In re Thomas (1977) 3 FPPC Ops.
30.

Unlike City officials, a county or state official who
receives an Airport parking pass would be receiving a gift from
the City, unless the use of the pass is restricted to occasions
when the county or state official traveled primarily for a City
purpose. An Airport parking permit given to a county or state
official would ordinarily be subject to the gift reporting, limitations and
prohibitions, and conflict of interest disqualification
provisions.

As to Airport parking permits issued to Federal officials,
the Fair Political Practices Commission takes no position. The
Political Reform Act does not govern transactions between local or
state agencies and Federal officials. We suggest you seek advice
from the appropriate Federal agency regarding the proposed
issuance.

I trust the above answers your question. If you have need of further information, please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[signature]
by: Susan L. Borrow
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:SLB:ken

sections.question: Are there reporting and disqualification obligations for city, county, and Federal public officials who receive parking permits for parking at the Airport while on official business?
sections.conclusion:
Parking permits given to city officials for parking at the airport while on official city business would not be considered gifts and would be neither reportable nor relevant to conflict of interest disqualification. Parking permits given to county or state officials are reportable gifts to the recipients. Permits given to Federal officials are not within the jurisdiction of the Act.

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section 18329(c)(3).)
sections.facts: The Office of the City Attorney in the City of San Jose, California ("City") is developing an ordinance regarding payment of fees by persons parking at the Airport. The Airport is a City department. The City is considering granting exemptions to payment of parking fees to city, county, state, and Federal public officials who park at the Airport while travelling on official business. Your concern is whether receipt of such parking permits would subject the recipient to reporting and disqualification provisions of the Act. At present, City officials who travel on official business are reimbursed by the City for their parking fees.
sections.analysis:
We have previously advised that parking passes given to public officials are reportable gifts when they are given to the official by a governmental entity other than the one that employs the official. Bailey Advice Letter, I-91-424.

However, in the circumstances herein, the Airport is a City department, and City officials who park at the Airport while travelling on City business are reimbursed for any out-of-pocket parking fees incurred. For City officials using the Airport parking facilities while on official business, reimbursement for parking is in the nature of repayment for a personal outlay of funds to cover a cost that is related to the officials' duties. Providing a permit for City officials to use while parking at the Airport while on City business would serve the same purpose as the current reimbursement procedure. The permit, therefore, would not be a gift, and would be neither reportable as such nor relevant to a conflict of interest analysis. In re Thomas (1977) 3 FPPC Ops. 30.

Unlike City officials, a county or state official who receives an Airport parking pass would be receiving a gift from the City, unless the use of the pass were restricted to occasions when the county or state official traveled primarily for a City purpose. An Airport parking permit given to a county or state official would ordinarily be subject to those provisions relating to gifts in the Act, including reporting, limitations and prohibitions, and conflict of interest disqualification provisions.

As to Airport parking permits issued to Federal officials, the Fair Political Practices Commission takes no position. The Political Reform Act does not govern transactions between local or state agencies and Federal officials. We suggest you seek advice from the appropriate Federal agency regarding the proposed issuance.

I trust the above answers your question. If you have need of further information, please do not hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Parking permits given to county or state officials are reportable gifts to the recipients. Permits given to Federal officials are not within the jurisdiction of the Act.
